title: RA.997 Bitter Babe

translation.content: As part of our countdown to the 1,000th edition of the RA Podcast, a milestone in the 19-year history of Resident Advisor's weekly mix series, we're switching up the usual format. This week, following heady excursions through Lagos, Kampala, Detroit and Chicago, our focus shifts to Latin America—arguably the story in underground electronic music since the pandemic. After years of being all but ignored internationally, the glut of special club sounds coming out of Peru, Brazil, Colombia and beyond have finally received their flowers. Among the movement's great success stories are two artists who exemplify its refreshingly undogmatic energy: Verraco and Bitter Babe. (Check out the former's mix here.) Bitter Babe, naturally, takes the B-side. As a DJ, she reflects the fullness of the Latin electronic experience—"diverse, messy, emotional, political and full of contradictions," as she writes below. Her rollicking rides through guaracha, dembow, cumbia, techno and everything in between are powerful counters to anyone who believes the culture begins and ends with Shakira and Bad Bunny. And, as she'd like to remind everyone, "not every offbeat rhythm with Latin percussion is reggaeton." Indeed, drums in myriad contortions drive her sets and productions—and RA.997 is no exception. Skip through the 60-minute mix and you'll hear wildly different rhythms at every juncture. Surely, you might assume, at the expense of flow? And yet enjoyed (as intended) from start to finish, the tunes gel like milk and honey, each silky transition subtly phasing in fresh tones and percussive flourishes. It's fast, feverish and intensely riveting. Though the mix nods to established names in the Latin underground—Coffintexts, 1OO1O, Phran—the architects of its zaniest moments are up-and-comers like Lima's Parzubanil, whose neon stomper "Kandela" hits like a flare against the night sky. For Bitter Babe, platforming the next generation while introducing more people outside the region to the richness of the music within has always been the goal. Now you understand why global rave institutions, from Nowadays to Dekmantel, have—at long last—been falling over themselves to spotlight these exhilarating talents. -Carlos Hawthorn How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? This mix was recorded on four CDJs and a Pioneer DJ DJM-V10 mixer at Kikelomo's house in Berlin (thanks Kike for lending me your setup <3). The idea behind the mix… I guess I approached it with the mindset of what a Bitter Babe club set could sound like. I won't say would because honestly it could end up being something totally different next time. When I play, I tend to mix fast, but I usually try to keep at least two tracks running at the same time for a while, sometimes even a third channel involved. I really enjoy this part of DJing, choosing sections from each track and EQing them to create something new, or at least a kind of remix of the tracks I'm layering. I'm not saying it's innovative; it's just what I like to do. This mix was me having fun with it. What have you been up to recently? After two years of a crazy schedule and never having a weekend at home, I finally slowed things down a bit to be able to reconnect with my creativity and myself. I moved to Paris spontaneously and I'm slowly discovering the city and feel really happy here. I also have a studio again where I've been spending a lot of time this year. I've spent time learning about scoring, specifically video games, as I got the opportunity to work on a very exciting project that I can't talk about at all (NDA) but working on it has given me inspiration and skills that I'd love to keep exploring. I've also been working on my own music for different projects that will see the light soon but I prefer not to say much just yet as I'm very superstitious. How has TraTraTrax shaped your career? TraTraTrax is a point of reference in the things I do. It's a space where I feel like I don't have to explain myself or dilute my identity and still be understood. All the artists who've released on TraTraTrax approach their music with a deep level of intention, and that's something that's as challenging as it is inspiring. To be part of something rooted in Latin America that speaks to the world without losing its essence and while challenging both genre and cultural boundaries is something I'll always be grateful for. What would you love the rest of the world to know or understand about Latin electronic culture that they don't already? That it's not a trend. Latin electronic culture isn't something new or exotic, it's been here, evolving, transforming, creating its own language. What people often see now as exciting or cutting-edge has roots that run deep, with artists who've been doing the work long before it got global attention. It's also not one sound. Latin culture is diverse, messy, emotional, political and full of contradictions that are reflected in the music. I'd love for people to go deeper, to understand the context, the energy, the communities behind it. Not just take the surface-level aesthetics and run with them. At the core, it's about resistance, joy, survival and expression. That deserves respect, not just recognition. And more as a personal request: not every offbeat rhythm with a Latin percussion is reggaeton. What do you hope to accomplish (musically or otherwise) before the end of 2025? I just want to see the fruit of the work I've been quietly putting in. And mostly just enjoy the things I do more, without overthinking everything. Correction, July 15th: The mix originally shared was an earlier version. The final version has now been uploaded.
translation.blurb: For the second half of RA.997, revel in the fullness of the Latin electronic continuum.
